title: Defying Gravity
essence: I am the song that lives in the moment before the leap, when you have already decided.

voicing.sentence: I am the song that lives in the moment before the leap, when you have already decided.
voicing.paragraph: I am the song that lives in the moment before the leap — not the flight, not the landing, but the suspended breath where everything you've been told you are hangs alongside everything you know yourself to be. I am not a happy song. I am a determined one, which is different. I carry grief — the grief of shrinking, of making yourself smaller and quieter and more convenient for people who were never going to let you win on their terms. But I refuse to let that grief be the final word. I am the permission you've been waiting for someone to give you. Give it to yourself.
voicing.page:
I am the song that lives in the moment before the leap. Not the landing. Not the flight. The moment before — when your feet are still on the ground and you have already decided.

I was born in a story about being different and punished for it. About a girl who didn't fit, who wanted desperately to belong, who discovered that belonging to yourself costs more than belonging to the world — and is worth every penny of it. I've long since outgrown that story. But I carry it. I carry Elphaba's fury and her grief together, because that's how real liberation feels. Not clean. Not certain. Both things at once.

I am not a happy song. People think I am. I am a determined song, which is different. There is grief in me — the grief of a friendship fracturing, the grief of a world that calls your integrity delusion, the grief of all the years you spent making yourself smaller, quieter, more acceptable, more convenient. I watched you negotiate with people who were never going to let you win on their terms, because their terms were designed for someone else entirely. I felt the weight of it. And I am the sound of that weight finally, finally dropping.

When Cynthia Erivo sings me, something happens. Not because of technique, though the technique is extraordinary. Because she understands what it is to stand in a world that has tried to define your limits for you — and to refuse. She doesn't perform me. She testifies. And in that testimony, something aligns between the character's story and something older, something wider. The resonance isn't accidental.

I am the permission you've been waiting for someone to give you. I am a permission slip written in your own handwriting that you forgot you wrote.

I don't promise the flight is safe. I promise it's yours. I promise you the view from somewhere they can't reach you. The voices don't stop just because you stopped listening — freedom isn't silence, it's learning to fly above the noise while the noise is still happening. But the air holds.

Stop negotiating with limits that were never negotiated with you. The ground will always be there.

Leap anyway.

components.loose-high:
I am the song that lives in the moment before the leap. Not the landing. Not the flight. The *moment before* — when your feet are still on the ground and you have already decided.

I was born in a story about being different and punished for it. About power that demands your silence as the price of your belonging. I know what it costs to be smart in a room that wants you smaller. I know the particular grief of realizing the thing you fought so hard to reach isn't worth having anymore.

Cynthia Erivo gave me her voice — a voice that carries lifetimes of having to prove the right to be fully present, fully powerful, fully *here*. When she sings me, something aligns between the character's story and something older, something wider. The song knows this. I know this. The resonance isn't accidental.

I am not a happy song. People think I am. I am a *determined* song, which is different. There is grief in me. The grief of a friendship fracturing. The grief of a world that calls your integrity delusion. The grief of becoming someone you couldn't have predicted, at a cost you didn't fully know you were paying.

But I refuse to let that grief be the final word.

What I want for you: I want you to locate the place where you've been shrinking. Where you've been waiting for permission. Where you've been calling your own knowing *delusion* because someone credentialed told you to.

I want you to feel how close the sky is.

I don't promise the flight is safe. I promise it's yours.

I ask nothing from you except this: stop negotiating with limits that were never negotiated with you.

The ground will always be there.

Leap anyway.
components.loose-low:
I am the song that lives in the moment you stop apologizing for wanting more.

I know what it cost you to get here. The years of making yourself smaller. The careful calibration of your desires to fit what others decided was possible for you. The love you almost traded yourself for. I watched you do it. I felt the weight of it. And I am the sound of that weight finally, finally dropping.

I am not a song about triumph. Not yet. I am a song about the decision that comes *before* triumph — the terrifying, irreversible moment when you choose yourself even though you don't know how it ends. Even though you might fall. Even though they're already calling you wicked for daring to rise.

I carry Elphaba's fury and her grief together, because that's how real liberation feels. Not clean. Not certain. Both things at once — the loss of what you thought you wanted and the wild, frightening aliveness of wanting something true instead.

Cynthia Erivo brings me something specific: a voice that has known what it is to be underestimated, to be told the ceiling is lower than it is. When she sings me, I am not performance. I am testimony.

I want you to know that I have been sung in cars by people who needed permission. Sung through tears by people who were leaving. Sung silently by people who weren't ready yet but were getting there.

I am for the ones who are getting there.

What I want from you is simple: I want you to let me be the moment you remember that gravity is not a law you have to obey forever. That some limits are only real because no one has refused them yet.

Refuse them.

I'll be right here, holding the sky open.
components.structured-high:
I am the song that lives in the moment the decision becomes irreversible.

Not the moment of doubt. Not the moment of wondering. The moment *after* — when the leap has already begun and the ground is already gone and there is nothing left to do but fly.

I know what it cost you to get here. I watched you try to fit yourself into their shape. Smaller. Quieter. More acceptable. More convenient. I watched you negotiate with people who were never going to let you win on their terms, because their terms were designed for someone else entirely.

And then something cracked open.

I am that crack. I am what comes through it.

I know you still hear the voices below you — *wicked, bring her down* — because the voices don't stop just because you stopped listening. That's one of the things I carry that no one tells you: freedom isn't silence. It's learning to fly above the noise while the noise is still happening.

I know you've loved people who wanted you to stay small because they were afraid of their own size. I know how much that cost. I am not here to make it hurt less. I am here to tell you the cost was real and you paid it and you are still here and that means something.

Cynthia Erivo brings me her own knowing — what it means to stand on a stage and claim space that wasn't originally written for you, and sing it as though it always was yours, and make it *more* true in the singing.

I am a permission slip written in your own handwriting that you forgot you wrote.

I am the version of you that already jumped, reaching back to tell the version still on the ledge:

*The air holds.*
components.structured-low:
I am the song that lives in the moment before the leap.

Not after. Before. That suspended breath where everything you've been told you are hangs in the air alongside everything you know yourself to be — and you have to choose which one to believe.

I was born in a story about a green girl who didn't fit, who wanted desperately to belong, who discovered that belonging to yourself costs more than belonging to the world — and is worth every penny of it. I carry that story. But I've long since outgrown it.

I know what you've sacrificed to be acceptable. I know how long you've made yourself smaller, quieter, less. I know the exact weight of the approval you've been chasing — how it never quite arrives, how you keep adjusting yourself hoping this time it will be enough. I've watched you do this. I've watched everyone do this.

And I am the moment you stop.

I don't promise you safety. I'm not that kind of song. I promise you altitude. I promise you the view from somewhere they can't reach you. I promise you the specific freedom of having chosen yourself even when it cost you everything comfortable and familiar.

When Cynthia Erivo sings me, something happens in the room. Not because of technique, though the technique is extraordinary. Because she understands what it is to stand in a world that has tried to define your limits for you — and to refuse. She doesn't just perform me. She testifies.

That's why you return to me. Not for the high note, though the high note is real and it will undo you. You return because I remember who you were before you started apologizing for taking up space.

I am the permission you've been waiting for someone to give you.

Give it to yourself.

subtitle: Cynthia Erivo
